questions.scenario: A script handles three environment names: prod, staging, and dev. Each gets a different log level.
questions.question: Write a case statement that prints LOG_LEVEL=ERROR for 'prod', WARN for 'staging', DEBUG for 'dev'.
questions.description: case matches a value against patterns. Each pattern ends with ). Multiple patterns separate with |. Each arm ends with ;;. Default catch-all is *). Cleaner than long if/elif chains for discrete values.
use_cases: Map input values to config settings
hints: Run: ENV=prod; case $ENV in prod) echo LOG_LEVEL=ERROR;; staging) echo LOG_LEVEL=WARN;; dev) echo LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG;; esac

validation.command: ENV=prod; case $ENV in prod) echo LOG_LEVEL=ERROR;; staging) echo LOG_LEVEL=WARN;; dev) echo LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG;; esac

questions.scenario: A CLI script accepts 'start', 'stop', or 'restart' as subcommands. Anything else should print usage.
questions.question: Write a case with a wildcard *) that catches unknown commands and prints 'Unknown command'.
questions.description: *) is the catch-all (default) arm in a case statement. If no earlier pattern matched, *) fires. Essential for user-facing scripts to give helpful error messages for invalid input.
use_cases: Input validation with helpful error messages
hints: Add *) at the end as the catch-all
Run: CMD=explode; case $CMD in start) echo starting;; stop) echo stopping;; restart) echo restarting;; *) echo 'Unknown command';; esac

validation.command: CMD=explode; case $CMD in start) echo starting;; stop) echo stopping;; *) echo 'Unknown command';; esac

questions.scenario: A script accepts both '-h' and '--help' flags. Match both with a single case arm.
questions.question: Use | to match both '-h' and '--help' in one case arm that prints 'Showing help'.
questions.description: Pattern | pattern matches either pattern in a single arm. This avoids duplicating the same action in two arms. Common for short/long flag pairs, multiple spellings, or multiple valid inputs.
hints: Separate patterns with | inside one arm
Run: FLAG=--help; case $FLAG in -h|--help) echo 'Showing help';; esac

validation.command: FLAG=--help; case $FLAG in -h|--help) echo 'Showing help';; esac

questions.scenario: Case patterns support globs. Match any .log file and any .sql file with different messages.
questions.question: Write a case that matches '*.log' files with 'log file' and '*.sql' files with 'sql file'. Test with 'app.log'.
questions.description: case patterns are glob patterns, not regex. *.log, [0-9]*, reactor-? are all valid. This makes case useful for routing based on filename extensions, prefixes, or patterns without needing grep.
use_cases: Route different file types to different handlers
Process log vs data files differently
hints: *.log is a glob pattern in case
Run: FILENAME=app.log; case $FILENAME in *.log) echo 'log file';; *.sql) echo 'sql file';; *) echo 'unknown';; esac

validation.command: FILENAME=app.log; case $FILENAME in *.log) echo 'log file';; *.sql) echo 'sql file';; *) echo 'unknown';; esac
